# ARC-QUIC-RFC9002-0006 - QUIC RFC 9002 Appendix A Helper Recovery-State Architecture

## Purpose

Split the helper-backed RFC 9002 Appendix A recovery-state subset from the broader recovery-runtime artifact so it can be verified without claiming sender-owned packet tracking, PTO probe sending, or key-discard lifecycle behavior.

## Scope

This design covers only the Appendix A clauses that map cleanly to existing helper surfaces: recommended recovery constants, RTT estimator updates, helper-level loss/PTO timer selection, and helper-level loss-delay calculations. It does not claim the deferred Appendix A sender ledger, packet-record ownership, timer ownership, PTO probe emission, anti-amplification unblocking, or key-discard cleanup clauses.

## Design Summary

The Appendix A helper subset stays below the connection runtime. `QuicRecoveryTiming` owns the recommended packet/time/granularity constants, packet-threshold and time-threshold loss helpers, PTO backoff arithmetic, earliest loss-time selection, no-in-flight PTO selection, application-data PTO gating before handshake confirmation, application-data max_ack_delay/backoff inclusion, and timer cancellation/selection decisions. `QuicRttEstimator` owns first and later RTT sample updates, min RTT maintenance, post-confirmation ack_delay capping, adjusted RTT selection, and weighted smoothed_rtt/rttvar updates. `QuicAckGenerationState` supplies the helper evidence for largest acknowledged packet tracking and ACK-frame range behavior, and `QuicCongestionControlState` keeps the existing recovery constants and congestion helper benchmarks visible where Appendix A references congestion callbacks.

## Key Components

- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicRttEstimator.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicRecoveryTiming.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicAckGenerationState.cs
- src/Incursa.Quic/QuicCongestionControlState.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9002/REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SAP2-0001.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9002/REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SAP7-0009.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9002/REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SAP8-0006.cs
- tests/Incursa.Quic.Tests/RequirementHomes/RFC9002/REQ-QUIC-RFC9002-SAP10-0002.cs
- benchmarks/QuicRttEstimatorBenchmarks.cs
- benchmarks/QuicCongestionControlBenchmarks.cs

## Data and State Considerations

The helper subset owns only in-memory calculation and estimator state. It does not persist recovery state, own packet ledgers, mutate connection runtime timers, emit probe packets, or discard key-owned packet spaces.

## Edge Cases and Constraints

- The helper subset must not claim ownership of packet records or sent_packets cleanup.
- Timer selection helpers can choose deadlines or cancellation outcomes, but runtime timer arming and PTO probe emission remain outside this slice.
- ApplicationData PTO remains gated before handshake confirmation at the helper boundary.
- Fuzz/property top-offs exercise deterministic input ranges over RTT adjustment, timer priority, and loss-delay arithmetic without weakening protocol behavior.

## Alternatives Considered

- Close all of RFC 9002 Appendix A in one artifact: rejected because SAP1, SAP4, SAP5, SAP6, SAP8-0005, SAP9, and SAP11 still require fuller sender, timer, probe, or key-discard runtime ownership.
- Treat the existing broad `ARC-QUIC-RFC9002-0001` as sufficient closure evidence: rejected because it verifies too much at once and still references deferred runtime-owned clauses.
- Add a new recovery runtime layer before closing the helper subset: rejected because the 23 selected clauses already map to stable helper APIs and can be proven without broadening runtime behavior.
- Edit benchmark source in this slice: rejected because the current helper behavior is already instrumented by existing RTT and congestion benchmark suites, and benchmark implementation changes are being handled separately.

## Risks

- If future work reads this helper closure as a full Appendix A recovery engine, sender/runtime gaps could be hidden behind helper evidence.
- If timer helper results are treated as timer ownership, the runtime may appear more complete than it is.
- If internal helper benchmarks are compared to public transport throughput, performance claims will be misleading.
